speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Linda Burr. I'm interviewing Joseph Talpert in Johannesburg, South Africa. The date is June 13, 1996-- tape 2. Joseph, your parents left you in Toulouse and went back to Antwerp. How did you feel when you-- when you were left in-- in Toulouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: To be quite honestly, I was not upset about that. I would have liked to go with them, but said-- as said previously, I found it dangerous. And I was thinking about getting out from France, joining the-- to get to England at the time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which, of course, didn't work out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you alone? Was anyone with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was alone, but very-- very shortly afterwards, my cousin managed to join me, cousin Kowalski, Grisha Kowalski. And we were together then, for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay in Toulouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Toulouse, I stayed up in a flat what my parents took when they came. But I wasn't so long in Toulouse, because very soon, the French authorities forced us to leave. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because originally, we were in that flat. But then they started moving the refugees. And they sent us what they call [FRENCH], what means-- it's a French word what means that you are allowed to go to some spot and not move further. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you're under supervision of the gendarme, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what they have in-- in most places in France. They were in charge of security. And you must report to them once a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you together with your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only in Toulouse. Because I was sent away, and then he went away to the south of France, where eventually, he found refuge in a farm near Aix-en-Provence, what is not very far from Marseilles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were moved from Toulouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toulouse to Luchon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I went by train. And I had to report to the gendarme.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you escorted by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't escorted. But they gave me what they call a [FRENCH], that you're allowed to go. In case they check in the train, or whatever it is, they shouldn't worry me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it to Luchon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toulouse to Luchon-- I would say it was a couple of hours by train. That's on the Spanish border by the Pyrenees.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the other people with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were people there, quite a-- a lot of refugees-- not from Belgium. From Belgium, there might have been just one, a few people, but mostly French people from Paris, from other parts. And quite a number of Jews were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a refuge particularly for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't. This was a really a-- a winter resort, that place, what they used for the facilities there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I managed to get some accommodation. And I was there quite a while, till eventually, I got to notice I must leave that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been there about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do while you were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Less than a year-- there wasn't much to be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you support yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used-- I had a little bit money on me. And I used to get a little grant from the Red Cross from-- through America. It came from England, really. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That time, life wasn't so expensive. If you're not extravagant, you could live-- live-- you could live on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that, Joseph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 19-- towards the end of 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in Luchon for about a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till-- about a year, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try and do some work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I tried to get away. And I tried to cross the Spanish border, the Pyrenees, but we were caught. At the border, when we came to Spain, we thought they'd let us through. And we managed to bribe the-- the Spanish guards there. And they let us return without turning us over to-- to the Germans. I was-- I went with another man there, in fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: With one other man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a man from Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish man also.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you try and escape? What-- on-- on foot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, by foot. Well, the Pyrenees was-- you know, because the Pyrenees, it's feasible there. There's some parts what are not so complicated. We were not going in the high peaks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you-- well, did you leave at night? How-- how did you try and escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took a couple of hours till we came to the other side of Spain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, what happened when you met the gendarme?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- then I went back to Luchon. And then, eventually, we were notified that we had to leave-- that I had to leave at least, not we.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you have to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had to go in Lacaune. It was quite a long way, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was also train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was at Lacaune?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a town, a little French town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I managed to get some accommodation and stay there for a while. And that's where I got in touch with some other French people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in there, we remained a day till about the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you actually in hiding in Lacaune?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's when I had my-- changed my papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got French identity papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that, Joseph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- in France-- at least in the country-- it's-- it's quite an easy process. You go to what they call, in French, a [FRENCH]. Because in French, there is a-- [FRENCH] is a government business. Only they are allowed to sell. And they give you the forms, the ID papers. You must get your ID papers. And these papers you must take to the [FRENCH], what is the mayor's place. You fill it out. And it must be stamped and identified by them with photo and fingerprints. This, of course, is feasible in little places like this. You couldn't do it in Paris where you would have to go to the prefecture. They wouldn't trust you to do it. But there, you could bring your own papers. And they identify it, and they make it legal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he take a false name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I looked in a-- in a phone directory. I want to take an area what shouldn't be suspicious. And I took an area in the north of France what occupied by the German. Because whatever it is, the Vichy were not checking what is there, the Vichy authorities. And I took a name and an address from there. And that's what I used for my papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What name did you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took the name of Thibault. Joseph Thibault I took. I wanted to have the same initials.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. 'Thibault' is T-H-I-B-A-U-L-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the officials know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't take it for-- they were not suspicious about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people who were doing the same thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: There might be other people, but they wouldn't say so. I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were on you own?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on my own there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, well, I got some contact. And I think they helped me around, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What contact, Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people were connected with the French Resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make that contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a lady what I met to be honest. She was, what they call in France, a letter box. A letter box is a-- a mailbox for the Resistance, you know? Messages and all this, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- just by-- I met her at a-- I stayed at a little hotel, and she was staying, also, there. And we became friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she offer you some assistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: She offered me-- she had a lot of contacts. And this helped me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually become part of the underground, or did they help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not altogether. I helped in a few little incidents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, incidents-- moving the items and various things what I had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what you had to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I wouldn't say it was armed incidents, but moving the documentation and other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Luchon?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had to go to-- I went to Toulouse, back, you know, and other places-- not Toulouse. Where is-- [FRENCH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you actually helping other people with papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Indirectly. Because there were some German raids also in the area, you know? And the marquis came down. And there were various things were to be fixed. By having connection, they gave you information, what to do and where to go. And I wouldn't see the German Wehr in that area particularly in force. They were not. But they used to-- they used to come down occasionally, checking. Because it was a little locality what they were not very much interested in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever feel in danger of being discovered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, but there's very little to say. Because when, at some stage, the whole town was, like, encircled, I managed to find some old Frenchman who-- and I stayed with him there. And he-- then he hid me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In his house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a house, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't long, because these raids were only-- they were coming and going. They were not searching entirely. They were going to big towns that would have had maintenance and everything. But in those little places, I suppose they didn't have the-- the manpower to do it. They just came through. They sent a squad or some few vehicles they send, and then they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was a particular house where the French guy was.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in a room? In an attic? Or where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a house. In fact, it was a double-level house, you know, in the hill there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other times when you had to hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not especially. I was in-- I was there in that area, but not especially. Well, there are alarms more than once, but it worked out right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the end of the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say from about-- from '41 till '45-- end of '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during that time, you were hiding in different people's houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have to be in hiding always. It's only when the Germans came around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and where did you stay during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed-- I had a room there by a French guy there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what did you do during the days?

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't much to be done. I used to help occasionally in there, do a little manual work, but not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did people ever question whether you were Jewish or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not for Jewish. They might be inquisitive why I was there the whole time. But they didn't show it officially. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because there were a few other refugees-- not Jews. In fact, one family was Jewish. It was a fellow who made pictures in Paris. He was a-- a filmmaker. He was hiding there also, yes, with his family. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, do you remember any of the names of the people that you were hiding with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one was a man called Minot-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] M-I-N-O-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was my main factor there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever make contact with him after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, I don't suppose he would be alive at that time. He wasn't young at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it would be useless to try to make contact, because I'm sure he couldn't be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Joseph, during this time, did you ever try and contact your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. I got-- I was in contact, through one other way, with Morocco. They must have found my address. Because-- because when we left Belgium, we made some arrangements where we could eventually hide. One, in fact, was-- I kept, and I can't remember. It was a wholesale firm what used to be a customer of my uncle, a Jewish wholesaler, and also, a cousin we had in Boston. I would imagine, rather, through Boston, we got-- they got my address.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your family write to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wrote occasionally, yeah, from Morocco. Well, that's the only contact I used to get still, from Belgium. I couldn't get news. And-- and then they were deported. And that was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the family in Morocco?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Morocco there was-- there were two of my aunties with their family, and also my granny.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they come to be in Morocco?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to Morocco. But when they left Belgium, they landed, eventually, in Bayonne, what is towards Spain and Atlantic Sea. And they managed to get on a boat what was still leaving at that time. Where that boat was going, nobody knew. Eventually, the boat landed in Morocco, French Morocco. Well, now it's independent, but that time, it was French still.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when the war started with the East and all this. And part of the fleet managed to go. Part of the fleet were-- was destroyed, the French fleet. Another part didn't listen to the orders. And they went to-- to Africa, you know? And that's where they landed eventually-- landed, eventually, in Marrakesh, and then to Mogador, where they also stayed till the end of the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were also afraid at the time, because the German were battling to get hold of Africa at that point and eventually defeat it. When Rommel was defeated, then they felt more secure. Joseph, did you ever hear from your family in Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In wartime? No more. I got some message at the beginning, and that was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like, one or the letter came through, you know? This was when I was still in Toulouse. And then-- well, I suppose, first, letters were intercepted, you know? And you couldn't get through. And that was the end. I never heard, afterwards, from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the letter that you got in Toulose, what-- what-- what did that say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time-- well, they-- they wrote carefully already, at that time, things [? you ?] [? couldn't ?] [? right ?.] At the beginning, it seemed-- things seemed to be all right. This was at the first stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they got back to Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got back. And I don't know what they managed to do exactly, because it didn't last long, unfortunately. Then they had to get-- they went into hiding. As I-- as I mentioned to you, we had a very faithful castle what we trusted 100%. Because we helped them, from nothing, to build up a tremendous business. And I caught, in my information, these people betrayed them. In fact, when I went back to Belgium after the war, when I came to know what was right and wrong, I wanted to make a court case to claim some damage just to have moral satisfaction. But they-- they declared themself insolvent. And the Belgium lawyers-- if you claim any amount, whatever it is, so many percent you must pay to the government even if you get money. But once they declare themself insolvent, there was no purpose to make any claim. I made a moral claim, what I called one franc, what means nothing. It's just a moral claim. And I couldn't do anything that means-- I couldn't get any satisfaction. Those people are still there. They've got their business-- never mind where they declared insolvent. And whatever they could steal, they stole. There were surely other things. I know their name. I went to see them. They put a big act up. And-- and that's how far it went. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And maybe if they would have played the game, my parents would have been alive today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, let's get back to the time in Luchon. You were there for quite a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in Luchon. Luchon was about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: A year. In Auvergne--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in that area--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you stayed for-- for a few years.

speaker: interviewee
text: There it was a longer time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any incidents that happened while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were quite incident. There was fighting incidents with fights with the German forces-- not exactly in the locality where I was, but in the area. We were quite-- periodically, I would say, there were incidents. It was a hilly area, forests, mountains there. And there were quite a number of hiding places. And the German didn't like to go too far there, because they didn't, always, secure. Then, incidents happened quite often.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you during this time? Did you-- did you witness any of them? Were you part of any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were some fatalities, of course. And then the German were leaving. Always, they-- they were not remaining. It was the big area. It wasn't-- it wasn't a big area, and it was no industry there. It was no business area mainly. Then, it returned to tranquility after they left-- typical country area. I wouldn't say that the population was pro-German. I wouldn't say that. They were very anti German, but not in, like, some area where they had what they call a lot of collaborators. This was, I would say, a-- I would say that more than 99% was anti-German. And we were always hoping, of course, Germany will get defeated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during this time, were you in constant touch with the underground movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your part? What-- what were you doing at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a very minor part.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just assisting. It was paperwork, moving items like-- mainly paperwork.

speaker: interviewer
text: In an office?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not office.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you make contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were people coming around, you know? You had to hand over documentation and things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the documentation for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, I didn't read it. But it was documentation about movement, of German movement, things happening in the area. Because they had-- in the mountains, there was quite a strong group of the Resistance, the Maquis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-Q-U-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And do you know what they were doing, overall, in the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was sabotage, you know? They were mining railways, bridges. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And Joseph, during this time, were you aware of the progress of the war? Did you know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, we were not allowed to listen to the radio. But it was what we called the de Gaulle movement, you know? They had the regular broadcast to France. That's when they used to play that famous tune from Beethoven, The Fifth Symphony. This was the opening tune.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you ever had any news of what was happening to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, we didn't know. But afterwards, when we heard the radio, we knew. We knew-- at the first stage, we didn't know what's happening. Nobody was aware. But coming from England, the news were there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was about '42. Well, it started early '42, you know, that we knew exactly-- not exactly. We knew quite a bit of news, information about the-- the fate of the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the other people in the area to this news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were upset about this. I wouldn't say everybody believed it. Because in fact, at some stage, when I managed to get contact with my cousin, what I was-- I went to see him in that farm where he was. It was an old man, the father. Then the son told him, well, you know, that man is hiding here because the German want to-- [INAUDIBLE] was, well if-- well, if the German want to kill him, that he deserves it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in his mentality, a country mentality, they couldn't imagine that the Germans could be so bad, right? They could understand this war, but not to kill people just for the sake because they're Jewish. Joseph, were you still trying to escape from France at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I didn't know how to do it at the time. I was trying to get contact, you know? But I couldn't get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the underground going to try and help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were going to try to help me, but I couldn't get a right way to go. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you make contact with any-- or did you become friendly with anyone at this time? Or were you totally on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I made contact. Well, first was a family what I knew there, what I knew-- not before, what I met there, and some French people what I met.
